versions.label: LSB
versions.text: "Or do you not know that all of us who have been baptized into Christ Jesus have been baptized into His death? Therefore we have been buried with Him through baptism into death, so that as Christ was raised from the dead through the glory of the Father, so we too might walk in newness of life."

context_lines: In **Romans 6**, Paul explains what union with Christ means for sin, death, and newness of life. The chapter's statements about baptism, slavery, freedom, and life under grace all serve the larger claim that those joined to Christ must no longer live as they once did.
explanation_lines: Going under the water is burial with Christ. Coming up out of the water is resurrection with Christ. The imagery only works with full immersion. You cannot bury someone by sprinkling dirt on their head.
Down into the water. Up out of the water. This is the consistent physical picture of baptism in the NT narrative.

Romans 6:3-4 established the picture already: *"we have been buried with Him through baptism into death, so that as Christ was raised from the dead through the glory of the Father, so we too might walk in newness of life."* Paul is not making a salvation mechanics argument here — he is making a sanctification argument. Baptism is the marker of the old self dying and the new self rising. It launches the believer into newness of life. This is not metaphor — it is spiritual reality enacted through the appointed act of obedience.
Having established what baptism actually accomplishes, it is just as important to be precise about what it does not — because both errors are common, and both distort the gospel.
